content:
8 min read
Just now

--

AI in healthcare didn’t arrive with a warning label. It arrived one convenient Tuesday at a time.
You feel it on a Tuesday. A dull ache under your ribs that wasn’t there last week. You’ve had aches before, and they went away, so you do the responsible thing and book a doctor. You’re not afraid. You’ve gone to this clinic for nine years.
Here’s the part nobody tells you: your doctor already knows what she’s going to say. Because she isn’t the one deciding anymore.
This is a story about AI in healthcare at the moment it stops being a helpful tool and quietly becomes the thing in charge. A machine will read your body in eleven seconds. It will be right about ninety percent of the time. The only question that matters is whether you’re in the ninety, or the ten.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A clinic that still smells human
The clinic smells like coffee and hand sanitizer, same as always. There’s a kiosk by the door now. There didn’t used to be. You check yourself in with your thumb.
The screen is friendly. It thanks you for coming, then asks you to rate your last visit. You remember that visit: a different doctor, an older man with silver glasses who ran three tests you didn’t think you needed and made you forty minutes late for work.
The kiosk offers a thumbs up and a thumbs down. You tap thumbs down. Low efficiency. It feels good, honestly, like leaving a bad review for a slow restaurant. The screen thanks you for your feedback. It really is polite.
Remember that thumb. It matters more than you think.
In the waiting room there’s a poster you’ve seen a hundred times. Ninety-four percent of patients agree: faster care, less waiting. You believe it. You’ve felt it. The visits are quicker now, less arguing, less waiting on results. A kid across the room is building a tower out of magazines. Someone’s coffee is going cold on the arm of a chair.
This is not a dystopia. It’s a Tuesday. That’s exactly why it works.
Dana at the front desk waves you through. She’s worked here longer than the kiosk has, and she still says good morning like she means it. Her son just started college. You’ve been coming here long enough to watch a receptionist’s kid grow up in a waiting room. The place felt human. It still smells human.
Nobody ever hands you a form that says the machine is in charge now. It just slowly becomes true. One convenient Tuesday at a time.
PRIORITY: LOW
Your doctor is Doctor Elena Reyes. You like her. She listens, which you’ve started to notice is rare. She’s the kind of doctor who looks at you, not the screen.
At least, she tries to. Today she’s already looking at the screen when you walk in. On the wall behind her is a display: your name, your file, and one word that was there before you sat down. PRIORITY: LOW.
You feel relieved. Low sounds good. Low sounds like nothing’s wrong. You don’t notice the way her jaw tightens when she reads it.
She asks the normal questions. When did it start. Does it hurt when you press here. You answer. She types. The screen updates in real time, already suggesting a plan: rest, fluids, come back in three weeks if it persists. She reads it out loud, but slower than the screen wrote it.
Then she does something the screen didn’t ask her to do. She presses her fingers under your ribs, on the left, and watches your face.
“Does that — “ She stops. “Hm.”
You don’t know what “hm” means. Neither does the machine. That’s the problem. The machine has never once said “hm.”
Nine minutes of red
It took the system eleven seconds to decide you were fine. Doctor Reyes has now spent nine minutes disagreeing. On the screen, those nine minutes have a color, and the color is red. A thin line appears at the bottom of the display: Deviation detected. Concordance impact pending.
You ask what that means. She smiles the way people smile when they’re deciding how much truth you can handle. “It means I’m taking too long,” she says.
She’s not taking too long. She’s the only person in the building actually looking at you.
She mutters something, half to herself. “I’ve got two flags left this quarter.” You don’t ask what a flag is. You will wish you had.
Here’s what a flag is. Every doctor is scored on how often they agree with the algorithm. They call it a Concordance Score.

• Agree with the machine, and your score stays healthy.

• Disagree, and you spend a flag.

• Run out of flags, and the system marks you an outlier.

• Outliers lose their patients. Then their license.
So your doctor is doing math right now. Not about your body. About her career. The screen says you are fine, and ninety percent of the time the screen is right. She knows that number. She helped build it, back when it was only advice.
That’s the part she can’t say out loud. The machine used to suggest. Now it decides. And nobody remembers voting on that.
The second opinion is the same machine
You try to be reasonable. You ask the obvious question: can’t we just get a second opinion?
Doctor Reyes almost laughs. Not because it’s funny. “The second opinion is the same system,” she says. “You appeal to the machine, and the machine grades its own homework.”
Only about two in every thousand people ever appeal at all. The system was built knowing that. It’s not a bug. It’s the business model.
You look at her differently now. She isn’t being difficult. She’s being brave, and it’s costing her something you can watch on the wall. Every second she spends on you, the number that pays her mortgage ticks down.
She turns the screen toward you and shows your scan from last year beside today’s. She points at a shadow that is bigger now than it was. “The system compared you to a million people who got better on their own,” she says. “It’s probably right.” Then, quieter: “But it’s never met you.”
That’s the moment the ache under your ribs stops feeling like nothing.
> The system compared you to a million people who got better on their own. But it’s never met you.
The friendly man from Care Quality
The door opens. It’s not Dana. It’s a man in a soft grey shirt with a lanyard, from something called Care Quality. He’s very friendly. He’s not here for you. He’s here because the screen called him. Doctor Reyes’s deviation pulled him in from down the hall.
“Everything alright in here?” He’s smiling. He glances at the wall. He sees the red line. He sees your PRIORITY: LOW. He sees a doctor spending a flag on a patient the system already cleared.
“You know your numbers this quarter,” he says to her. Gently. Like a friend. “You really want to spend one here?”
Nobody raises their voice. That’s what makes it terrifying. There is no villain in this room: just a very reasonable man, a very reasonable machine, and your very unreasonable doctor.
You want to believe this is one broken clinic. It isn’t. The same kind of system already runs quietly inside health plans covering millions of people. They were built to do prior authorization at scale, to decide in seconds what care gets approved. Somewhere along the way, deciding what to pay for became deciding what you need. The doctor didn’t lose the argument. The doctor was never invited to it.
The red line on the screen isn’t thin anymore. Concordance impact: SIGNIFICANT. The whole bottom of the display glows now, like a wound.
Two doors, and the choice is yours
Doctor Reyes looks at you, then at the screen, then at her flag count. She has two options.
She could sign off and send you home. Rest, fluids, three weeks. If you’re in the ninety percent, you’ll be fine and she keeps her career. If you’re in the ten percent, you’ll be back, much later than today.
Or she spends the flag. She overrides the machine and orders the scan the system said you don’t need. You get seen today. You maybe catch the thing that’s growing while it can still be caught. And she walks out of that room an outlier, one flag from the end of everything she trained for.
She turns to you. For the first time all day, the machine has nothing to say. It’s your call now. Not hers. Not the screen’s.
Option A: Tell her to override. Get your scan today, maybe save your own life, and spend the last good doctor in that building.
Option B: Trust the machine. Go home, protect her, and bet your body on a number that’s right ninety percent of the time.
Before you read on, sit in it for a second. What would you actually do?
The twist you didn’t see coming
You told her to override.
She didn’t hesitate. She pressed the flag. The screen turned fully red, then strangely went quiet. Somewhere, a number next to her name dropped. It will keep dropping.
They found it. Early. Small enough to treat, because you came in on the Tuesday it was still small. You are going to be okay. She is not.
On your way out, the kiosk asks you to rate your visit. Same thumbs up. Same thumbs down. The same screen where you sank a thorough doctor a year ago for running too many tests.
You finally understand what you did that day. The man with the silver glasses ran those tests because he looked at people, not screens. You gave him a thumbs down. You fed the machine that is now deleting Doctor Reyes.
That poster. Ninety-four percent of patients agree: faster care, less waiting. You were the ninety-four percent. You are the reason the flags keep getting rarer.
She had two flags this quarter. She spent one saving a patient who helped build the system that punishes her for it. The machine didn’t take your doctor away. You did. One thumb, one rating, one Tuesday at a time.
This part isn’t fiction
Systems like this already exist. Not in twenty years. Now.
UnitedHealth uses an algorithm called nH Predict, built by a company it bought called NaviHealth. A class action lawsuit says the tool was used to override doctors and cut off care for elderly patients. The same lawsuit says the company knew the tool had a roughly ninety percent error rate, and kept using it, because only about two in a thousand patients ever appeal. In March of 2026, a judge ordered the company to hand the algorithm over.
Right now, most of these systems still call themselves decision support. They advise. A human still signs. But the moment you score that human on how often they agree, you have quietly moved the decision. The doctor is still in the room. The doctor just isn’t deciding anymore.
So here’s the question. The next time a screen says you’re fine, and a tired human in front of you hesitates, who are you going to believe? And what will it cost the one who is willing to be wrong for you?
The turning point already happened. You just weren’t watching.
